skill.name: claude-code-guide
skill.description: A Chinese guide for learning Claude Code tools, workflows, MCP integration, hooks, REPL usage, and advanced development patterns.
skill.summary: Learn practical Claude Code workflows, tool usage, and advanced configuration patterns.
skill.author: 2025Emma

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer found many command, network, filesystem, environment, and sensitive-file patterns. Review shows most are documentation examples in a Claude Code guide, not executable skill logic, but several examples encourage risky operational behavior that needs a marketplace warning.

medium_findings.title: Secret Exposure in Troubleshooting Guidance
medium_findings.description: The guide tells users to check an API key by printing ANTHROPIC_API_KEY. This is not malicious code, but it can expose secrets in terminal history, logs, or shared screens.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is presented as troubleshooting guidance and directly references an API key environment variable. The risk is real if copied, although it is documentation rather than executed skill code.

medium_findings.title: Broad Filesystem Access Examples
medium_findings.description: The guide demonstrates adding sibling projects and the ~/.claude directory to Claude Code context. This can expose personal configuration or unrelated repositories if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly show expanded directory access, including a home-directory Claude configuration path. It appears instructional, but the data exposure risk is concrete.

medium_findings.title: Privileged and Background Shell Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The documentation includes examples that restart services with sudo, remove cache directories, and start background processes. These are legitimate operations but risky when copied into the wrong project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are shown inside workflow examples and do not prove malicious intent. They still carry operational risk because they affect services, processes, and filesystem state.

medium_findings.title: Local Log Mining Examples
medium_findings.description: The guide shows hooks reading Claude logs under the user home directory. This can be useful for learning, but it may process sensitive local activity data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file paths and shell pipeline are explicit, but the surrounding text describes analytics and learning workflows. The concern is privacy exposure, not confirmed exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Static Command Findings Are Mostly Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: Many external command detections are ordinary examples for file inspection, search, and Claude Code usage. They are not executed by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are clearly inside explanatory Markdown sections. They show commands such as wc, grep, and Read as learning material rather than hidden automation.

low_findings.title: Sensitive File Scanner Hits Include False Positives
low_findings.description: Some critical sensitive-file hits are caused by benign strings such as SECURITY.md and shell variable names, not Windows SAM database access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text does not reference a Windows SAM path. The scanner appears to have matched substrings in ordinary documentation and variable names.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review for override, pre-approval, ignore-instructions, and similar phrases found no evidence of a prompt injection attempt in the scanned files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The only suspicious phrase found was a benign command example for starting a fresh Claude session. No evidence found for instructions that try to override the auditor.

dangerous_patterns.title: Printing Secret Environment Variables
dangerous_patterns.description: The pattern echo $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY can reveal a secret value to logs or terminal history.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact environment variable appears in a troubleshooting list. The risk depends on user execution, but the exposure mechanism is direct.

dangerous_patterns.title: Adding Home Configuration to Workspace Context
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes examples that add ~/.claude and Claude configuration files to Claude Code context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are clear and intentional. They may be legitimate for power users, but they increase exposure of personal configuration and credentials.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Substitution With Network Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: A status line example uses shell command substitution and curl. Repeated execution could make network requests from the local environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line is an example and does not send secrets in the cited text. It still demonstrates automatic network activity inside shell substitution.

content.value_statement: Claude Code has many tools, commands, and workflow patterns that can be hard to learn. This guide organizes practical usage patterns, examples, and configuration guidance in one reference.
actual_capabilities: Explains Claude Code core tools, including REPL, Artifacts, Web Search, Web Fetch, and conversation tools.
Provides workflows for large file analysis, data exploration, algorithm validation, and quality assurance.
Documents slash commands, custom command files, hooks, status lines, and multi-agent collaboration patterns.
Shows MCP configuration concepts, server types, authentication patterns, and external system integration.
Includes troubleshooting, security review, and best-practice sections for Claude Code users.
limitations: The guide is documentation only and does not enforce safe command execution.
Many examples are generic and must be adapted before use in a real project.
Some examples use shell, filesystem, network, and environment-variable access that require user judgment.
The content is primarily Chinese, so non-Chinese readers may need translation support.

use_cases.title: Learn Claude Code Basics
use_cases.description: Use the guide to understand available tools, slash commands, permissions, and common workflows.

use_cases.title: Build Team Workflows
use_cases.description: Use the examples to design repeatable workflows for reviews, tests, hooks, and project memory.

use_cases.title: Configure Advanced Integrations
use_cases.description: Use the MCP, hooks, status line, and agent sections to plan advanced Claude Code setups.

prompt_templates.title: Find the Right Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Claude Code guide to recommend a simple workflow for analyzing a medium-sized project. Include the tools I should use first.

prompt_templates.title: Explain a Tool Group
prompt_templates.prompt: Summarize the guide sections about REPL, Artifacts, and Web Fetch. Explain when each tool is useful and what safety checks apply.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Project Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Using the guide, propose a Claude Code setup for a web application team. Include command files, hooks, review steps, and safe defaults.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Advanced Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare my Claude Code configuration against the guide. Flag risky shell, network, filesystem, and secret-handling patterns before suggesting improvements.

output_examples.input: I need to inspect a large Markdown file with Claude Code.
output_examples.output: The assistant recommends counting lines and words, mapping headings, sampling important sections, and then reading targeted ranges.

output_examples.input: I want to add custom commands for repeated project tasks.
output_examples.output: The assistant explains where command files belong, how to name them, and which project workflows should become reusable commands.

output_examples.input: I want to connect Claude Code to an internal tool.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines MCP server planning, authentication choices, permission review, and testing steps before enabling the integration.

best_practices: Review every shell, filesystem, network, and environment-variable example before running it locally.
Prefer project-scoped command files and hooks over home-directory changes when sharing workflows.
Treat the guide as a learning reference, then validate commands against official Claude Code documentation.
anti_patterns: Do not paste troubleshooting commands that print API keys or secrets into shared terminals or logs.
Do not grant Claude Code access to broad home directories without a clear reason.
Do not copy sudo, background process, or destructive cleanup examples without reviewing their project impact.

faq.question: Is this skill an executable tool?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill that provides guidance and examples for Claude Code usage.

faq.question: What language is the guide written in?
faq.answer: The guide is primarily written in Chinese, with many command and code examples.

faq.question: Does it support Codex users?
faq.answer: Yes. Codex users can use it as a reference when comparing Claude Code workflows with Codex workflows.

faq.question: Are the command examples safe to run?
faq.answer: Not automatically. Some examples use shell commands, network requests, filesystem access, and environment variables.

faq.question: What should I check before using MCP examples?
faq.answer: Check server commands, arguments, environment variables, permissions, and network destinations before enabling an MCP server.

faq.question: Why is there a security warning?
faq.answer: The guide includes powerful operational examples, including secret display, broad filesystem access, and privileged commands.
